front: Novel: set at 124 Bluestone Road, which is haunted by the ghost of the daughter the escaped slave protagonist killed to prevent her return to slavery
Novel: opens with the three-word sentence '124 was spiteful'
Novel: dedicated to 'Sixty Million and more'
Novel: inspired by the true story of the escaped slave Margaret Garner
Novel: the protagonist escapes the Sweet Home plantation, whose cruel overseer is known as Schoolteacher
Novel: the protagonist attacks Mr. Bodwin with an ice pick, mistaking him for Schoolteacher
Novel: the protagonist describes the scars on her back as a 'choke-cherry tree'
Novel: Paul D arrives at the haunted house and 'wants to put his story next to hers'; he is told that 'thin love is no love at all'
Novel: Schoolteacher's cruel overseers burn the slave Sixo alive
Novel: Halle smears butter all over his face after witnessing his wife's breast milk being stolen
Novel: follows Milkman Dead III, named after the janitor Freddie catches his mother breastfeeding him well into childhood
Novel: the theme of human flight permeates the work, which begins with Robert Smith jumping from the roof of Mercy Hospital
Novel: the protagonist discovers he is descended from a man named Solomon, who could fly
Novel: features a vengeful vigilante society named the Seven Days, of which the protagonist's friend Guitar Bains is a member
Novel: ends with the protagonist traveling to Shalimar, Virginia and leaping at his friend Guitar
Novel: the protagonist's aunt Pilate has no navel
Novel: a couple chooses names for their daughters — First Corinthians and Magdalene Called Lena — by randomly putting a pin in the Bible
Novel: Pecola Breedlove desires the title feature as a symbol of white beauty, inspired by a Shirley Temple mug
Novel: Pecola Breedlove is raped by her alcoholic father Cholly, and the resulting child dies after being born prematurely
Novel: marigolds fail to bloom in 1941 after the protagonist is impregnated, foreshadowing her baby's death
Novel: chapter titles are run-on sentences with no punctuation that parody the Dick and Jane readers, with first periods then spaces removed
Novel: Soaphead Church grants a young girl the title feature after tricking her into poisoning a dog named Bob
Novel: three prostitutes — China, Poland, and Miss Marie — live above the Breedloves' storefront apartment
Novel: narrator Claudia MacTeer dismembers a white doll that resembles Shirley Temple
Novel: Claudia MacTeer and her sister Frieda plant marigold seeds believing it will save the protagonist's child
Novel: set in Lorain, Ohio; the central character is blamed for killing Geraldine's favorite cat
Novel: after being compared to a foaling horse, Pauline 'Polly' screams during childbirth to 'let them people know having a baby was more than a bowel movement'
Novel: Darlene's dress becomes stained with muscadine juice after Aunt Jimmy's funeral
Novel: cosmetics salesman Joe Trace fatally shoots his young mistress Dorcas
Novel: Joe Trace's wife Violet gains the nickname 'Violent' for stabbing Dorcas's corpse at the funeral
Novel: Violet's husband's mistress shows up to her husband's funeral dressed in white
Novel: the protagonist Joe Trace traces his heritage to a homeless woman named Wild
Novel: begins with the line 'They shoot the white girl first' but never reveals which Convent resident is white
Novel: nine men from the all-Black town of Ruby, Oklahoma attack and kill women at the Convent
Novel: an oven has a disputed inscription referring to 'the Furrow of His Brow'
Novel: the stowaway Son has an affair with the Sorbonne-educated Jadine Childs on the Caribbean Isle des Chevaliers
Novel: the wealthy retiree Valerian Street's wife Margaret abused their son Michael
Novel: ends as its main character first crawls, then runs into the forest to join a band of blind, naked horsemen
Novel: the PTSD-afflicted veteran Shadrack leads the celebration of National Suicide Day each year on January 3rd
Novel: set in the neighborhood called the Bottom
Novel: the title character sleeps with her best friend Nel's husband and accidentally kills the boy Chicken Little
Novel: the title character's grandmother Eva allegedly lost her leg by purposely placing it on a railroad track for insurance money
Novel: set on the 17th-century Virginia estate of the rum trader Jacob Vaark
Novel: the enslaved Angolan Florens and the Native American servant Lina both survive a smallpox epidemic
Work: literary criticism exploring an 'Africanist presence' central to American literature; adapted from the 1990 Massey Lectures
Work: the chapter 'Romancing the Shadow' begins with an analysis of the 'white ghost' that appears at the end of Poe's Arthur Gordon Pym
Short Story: state wards Roberta and Twyla switch beds every night while living at a shelter and meet repeatedly throughout their lives
Short Story: the author's only short story — the narrative never reveals which of its two protagonists is Black and which is white
Author: first Black woman to win the Nobel Prize in Literature (1993)
Author: acquisitions editor at Random House who compiled The Black Book and championed diversity in publishing in the 1970s
Author: collaborated with Peter Sellars and Rokia Traore on the 2012 opera Desdemona and with Richard Danielpour on the opera Margaret Garner
Author: Nobel acceptance speech includes a parable in which young people ask a blind old woman whether a bird representing language is alive or dead
